title: Geneva Conventions 1949 — Common Article 3
text: Common Article 3 — the 'Convention in miniature' — applies to non-international armed conflicts and requires humane treatment of all persons not taking active part in hostilities, including members of armed forces who have laid down arms. It prohibits: violence to life and person including murder, mutilation, cruel treatment and torture; taking of hostages; outrages upon personal dignity especially humiliating and degrading treatment; and the passing of sentences and the carrying out of executions without previous judgment by a regularly constituted court. The ICRC is given a right of initiative to offer its services to parties to the conflict.

title: Geneva Convention III — Prisoner of War Treatment
source_name: Geneva Convention III relative to the Treatment of Prisoners of War
text: Geneva Convention III protects prisoners of war from the time they fall into enemy power until their final release. Article 4 defines POWs broadly to include members of armed forces, militia, volunteer corps, and resistance movements. Article 13 requires humane treatment at all times. Articles 17-20 govern interrogation: POWs need only provide name, rank, date of birth, and service number; no physical or mental torture, or coercion to obtain intelligence may be used. Article 118 requires repatriation without delay after cessation of active hostilities. ICTY applied GCIII standards to international armed conflict in Prosecutor v. Tadic (IT-94-1-A, 1999).

title: Geneva Convention IV — Protection of Civilians in Occupied Territory
source_name: Geneva Convention IV relative to the Protection of Civilian Persons in Time of War
text: Geneva Convention IV protects civilians during international armed conflict and occupation. Article 27 protects civilians in all circumstances; they must be treated humanely and protected against acts of violence, intimidation, insults and public curiosity. Article 47 prohibits any changes to the status of protected persons in occupied territory. Article 49 prohibits deportations and forcible transfers of protected persons. Article 55 requires the Occupying Power to maintain food and medical supplies. The ICJ in the Wall Advisory Opinion (2004) confirmed GCIV applies to the Occupied Palestinian Territory and that settlements violate Article 49(6).

title: Additional Protocol I 1977 — Distinction Principle
source_name: Additional Protocol I to the Geneva Conventions 1977
text: Additional Protocol I Article 48 codifies the basic rule of distinction: parties to the conflict must at all times distinguish between the civilian population and combatants, and between civilian objects and military objectives; they may only direct attacks against military objectives. Article 51 prohibits indiscriminate attacks including those using a method or means of combat that cannot be directed at a specific military objective, and attacks which may be expected to cause excessive civilian casualties relative to anticipated military advantage (proportionality rule). Article 57 requires all feasible precautions in attack to avoid or minimise civilian casualties. These rules reflect customary international law per ICRC Customary IHL Study Rules 1, 14, and 15.

title: Additional Protocol II 1977 — Non-International Armed Conflicts
source_name: Additional Protocol II to the Geneva Conventions 1977
text: Additional Protocol II develops and supplements Common Article 3 for non-international armed conflicts occurring in a State's territory between its armed forces and dissident armed forces or other organized armed groups that exercise sufficient control over territory. Article 4 provides fundamental guarantees for all persons who do not take a direct part or who have ceased to take part in hostilities. Article 13 prohibits attacks on the civilian population as such and acts of violence that would spread terror. Article 14 prohibits starvation of the civilian population as a method of warfare. Article 17 prohibits forced movement of civilians unless required for their security or imperative military reasons.

title: Martens Clause and ICRC Customary IHL Study
source_name: Martens Clause and Customary International Humanitarian Law
citation: ICRC Customary IHL Study 2005; Hague Convention IV Preamble 1907
text: The Martens Clause, first appearing in the 1899 Hague Convention II and restated in AP I Article 1(2), provides that in cases not covered by explicit treaty provisions, civilians and combatants remain under the protection and authority of the principles of international law derived from established custom, from the principles of humanity and from the dictates of public conscience. The ICRC Customary IHL Study (2005) identified 161 rules of customary IHL applicable in both international and non-international armed conflicts. Key customary rules include: Rule 1 (distinction), Rule 14 (proportionality), Rule 15 (precautions in attack), Rule 87 (humane treatment), Rule 90 (torture prohibition), Rule 97 (collective punishment prohibition). The ICJ in Legality of Nuclear Weapons (1996) confirmed the cardinal principles of distinction and Martens Clause as customary law.

title: Rome Statute 1998 — War Crimes Definition
source_name: Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court
text: Rome Statute Article 8 defines war crimes within the ICC's jurisdiction. These include grave breaches of the Geneva Conventions (wilful killing, torture, unlawful deportation, taking hostages, extensive destruction of property) and other serious violations of the laws and customs of war in international armed conflicts (intentionally directing attacks against civilians, using prohibited weapons, employing poison or poisoned weapons, using starvation as a method of warfare). Article 8(2)(c)-(f) covers war crimes in non-international armed conflicts. The ICC's Elements of Crimes further specify the contextual element requiring that the conduct take place in the context of and be associated with an armed conflict. The ICC convicted Thomas Lubanga Dyilo (ICC-01/04-01/06) for war crimes of conscripting child soldiers under AP II Article 4(3)(c).

title: Hague Regulations 1907 — Laws and Customs of War on Land
source_name: Hague Regulations respecting the Laws and Customs of War on Land
text: The 1907 Hague Regulations annex to Convention IV codify the laws and customs of land warfare reflecting customary international law at the time. Article 22 provides that belligerents do not have unlimited right to choose means of injuring the enemy. Article 23 prohibits certain means including treacherous killing, denying quarter, unlawful use of enemy flags and uniforms, and destruction of enemy property except where imperatively demanded by necessity. Article 25 prohibits attacks on undefended localities. Articles 42-56 govern occupied territories, requiring the Occupying Power to maintain public order and safety while respecting existing laws. The ICJ in Nicaragua v. United States (1986) and Legality of Nuclear Weapons (1996) confirmed the customary law status of the Hague Rules.

title: Ottawa Treaty 1997 — Anti-Personnel Mines
source_name: Convention on the Prohibition of the Use, Stockpiling, Production and Transfer of Anti-Personnel Mines
text: The Ottawa Treaty (Mine Ban Convention) entered into force in 1999 and has 164 States Parties. Article 1 prohibits under all circumstances the use, development, production, stockpiling, and transfer of anti-personnel mines and assistance with prohibited activities. Article 3 provides narrow exceptions for training in detection, mine clearance, and development of detection techniques. Article 5 requires States to destroy stockpiles within 4 years and clear mined areas within 10 years of entry into force. The treaty does not cover anti-vehicle mines or remotely-delivered munitions, which remain regulated by customary IHL proportionality and precaution rules. Major military powers including the US, Russia, and China have not ratified the treaty.

title: Chemical Weapons Convention 1993
source_name: Convention on the Prohibition of the Development, Production, Stockpiling and Use of Chemical Weapons
text: The Chemical Weapons Convention prohibits the development, production, acquisition, stockpiling, retention, transfer, and use of chemical weapons. Article I contains the general prohibitions. Article II defines chemical weapons broadly to include toxic chemicals and their precursors. Schedule 1 chemicals include nerve agents (sarin, VX, novichok agents) and blister agents (mustard gas). The OPCW (Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons) oversees verification through a comprehensive inspection regime. Following the 2018 Salisbury attack and 2013 and 2017 Syrian chemical attacks, the OPCW's investigative and attribution mandate was expanded. The use of chemical weapons is also prohibited under customary IHL as established in ICRC Rule 74.

title: ICRC Commentary — Proportionality Rule in Attack
source_name: ICRC Commentary on Proportionality (AP I Art. 51(5)(b))
citation: ICRC Commentary on Additional Protocols 1987, pp. 683-688
text: The proportionality rule under AP I Article 51(5)(b) prohibits attacks that may be expected to cause incidental loss of civilian life, injury to civilians, or damage to civilian objects that would be excessive in relation to the concrete and direct military advantage anticipated. The ICRC Commentary clarifies that 'concrete and direct' military advantage means a definite, not speculative advantage, close in time and place. The comparison is between expected civilian casualties and the military advantage; commanders must cancel or postpone attacks if proportionality is violated. The ICTY in Prosecutor v. Galic (IT-98-29-A, 2006) applied proportionality in the siege of Sarajevo context. The rule is accepted as customary IHL per ICRC Study Rule 14.

title: Tadic Case — ICTY Jurisdiction and IHL Scope
citation: ICTY, Prosecutor v. Tadic, IT-94-1, Appeals Chamber 1999
text: In Prosecutor v. Tadic (1999), the ICTY Appeals Chamber established that the conflict in the former Yugoslavia qualified as an armed conflict and that the Tribunal had jurisdiction. The case is foundational for several propositions: (1) the distinction between international and non-international armed conflicts matters for treaty application but many customary IHL rules apply to both; (2) the effective control test (Nicaragua) is not the appropriate standard for attributing acts to States — the overall control test applies to organized groups; (3) violations of Common Article 3 and laws and customs of war applicable in non-international conflicts can constitute crimes under customary international law. The case has been described as the ICTY's first substantive decision on the law of armed conflict.

title: Kunarac Case — Rape as War Crime and Crime Against Humanity
source_name: Prosecutor v. Kunarac, Kovac and Vukovic
text: In Prosecutor v. Kunarac (ICTY, 2002), the Appeals Chamber confirmed convictions for rape as a war crime under the laws and customs of war and rape as a crime against humanity. The judgment established that rape and sexual enslavement constitute torture under IHL when committed in the context of armed conflict. The definition of rape in international law does not require proof of force but requires absence of genuine consent. The judgment held that enslavement as a crime against humanity encompasses sexual enslavement and defined control over a person as the key element. This case significantly advanced the prosecution of sexual violence as a war crime and crime against humanity under international criminal law.

title: Nicaragua v. United States — Use of Force and IHL Customary Law
source_name: Case Concerning Military and Paramilitary Activities in and against Nicaragua
citation: Nicaragua v. United States, ICJ Reports 1986, p. 14
text: Nicaragua v. United States (ICJ, 1986) remains the leading authority on the prohibition of the use of force under Article 2(4) UN Charter and customary international law, and on the right of self-defence under Article 51 and customary law. The Court held that customary rules on use of force exist independently of the UN Charter. For self-defence to be lawful it must meet necessity and proportionality requirements. Regarding IHL, the Court confirmed the customary law character of Common Article 3, finding the US had violated minimum humanitarian standards by providing support to the Contras. The effective control test for State responsibility was established: the US was not responsible for Contra acts unless it directed those specific acts.

title: Legality of the Threat or Use of Nuclear Weapons — ICJ Advisory Opinion
source_name: Legality of the Threat or Use of Nuclear Weapons, ICJ Advisory Opinion
text: The ICJ's 1996 Nuclear Weapons Advisory Opinion confirmed the cardinal principles of IHL applicable to nuclear weapons: (1) the distinction principle between combatants and civilians; (2) the prohibition on causing unnecessary suffering; (3) the Martens Clause requiring that civilians and combatants remain protected by customary law even without treaty coverage. The Court found that the threat or use of nuclear weapons would generally be contrary to the rules of IHL, but left open in extremis circumstances of self-defence. The Court confirmed these principles as constituting 'intransgressible principles of international customary law.' The opinion is frequently cited for confirming the customary law status of IHL's basic principles.

title: Cluster Munitions Convention 2008
text: The Convention on Cluster Munitions prohibits the use, production, transfer, and stockpiling of cluster munitions that cause unacceptable harm to civilians. Article 2 defines cluster munitions broadly but excludes weapons with safeguards including self-destruction and self-deactivation features. Article 3 requires destruction of stockpiles within 8 years and clearance of contaminated areas within 10 years. 111 States Parties as of 2024. Major military users including US, Russia, China, Israel, Pakistan, India, and Saudi Arabia are not parties. Their use continues to be regulated under customary IHL rules of proportionality and precautions.
